metadata.title: OmegaFlow v13 Spectral Triple as Discrete Model for Quantum Gravity on H3 with Ramanujan Spectral Gap
metadata.description: OmegaFlow v13 Connes spectral triple (A, L2(H3), D) as discrete model for loop quantum gravity. Algebra A = K22 gate algebra (462-dimensional). Dirac operator spectrum lambda_n = kappa*(n+3/2) analogous to LQG area operator. Super-Ramanujan spectral gap kappa^2=2.567 provides Anderson delocalization. Selberg zeta Z(s) unifies Kleinian group geometry, Weyl law, and Ramanujan gap. Crown gate entering dH3 corresponds to Planck boundary where discrete quantum geometry transitions to continuum. kappa=1.60225455 from ORCID 0009-0003-2911-444X. ZKP commitment block 40840.

data.title: OmegaFlow v13 Spectral Triple as Discrete Model for Quantum Gravity on H3 with Ramanujan Spectral Gap

metadata.title: Prediction of Compaction Parameters and Permeability of Black Cotton Soil Stabilized with Fine Sand Using Artificial Neural Network
metadata.description: This study investigates the influence of particle size distribution on the compaction parameters and permeability of black cotton soil stabilized with fine sand and evaluates the predictive capability of Artificial Neural Networks (ANN). Seventy (70) soil samples were prepared by stabilizing black cotton soil with varying proportions of fine sand, with mix ratios ranging from 0% to 90% sand content to determine the optimal range for stabilization. Laboratory tests were conducted to determine the index properties, particle size distribution, compaction characteristics, and permeability of the stabilized soil. The experimental results showed that the addition of fine sand significantly modified the particle size distribution of the soil and improved its engineering behavior. The Optimum Moisture Content (OMC) ranged from 6.7% to 12.7%, while the Maximum Dry Density (MDD) varied between 1.76 g/cm³ and 2.02 g/cm³. The coefficient of permeability ranged from 1.24×10⁻⁹ to 1.57×10⁻⁷ m/s. The plasticity index of the stabilized soils ranged from 8.2% to 30.3%. The results further indicated that as the sand content increased from 0% to 90%, OMC decreased, MDD increased and permeability increased. The most important improvements were observed at higher sand contents, specifically in the range of 60% to 90% fine sand, where the reduction in the clay fraction improved particle packing, decreased compaction moisture requirements and increased the interconnected pore spaces responsible for improved drainage. An Artificial Neural Network model based on a multilayer perceptron architecture (8-10-10-3) was developed to predict the compaction parameters and permeability using Liquid Limit (LL), Plastic Limit (PL), Linear Shrinkage (LS), Plasticity Index (PI), Specific Gravity (Gs), Sand Content (S), Percentage Passing 0.45mm Sieve (P0.45) and Percentage Passing 0.075mm Sieve (P0.075) as input parameters. The model was trained and validated using normalized experimental data divided into training and testing datasets. The predictive performance of the ANN model was evaluated using statistical indicators including the coefficient of determination (R²), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), and Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE). The results demonstrated high predictive accuracy with Coefficient of Determination (R²) values of 0.977 for Optimum Moisture Content (OMC), 0.823 for Maximum Dry Density (MDD) and 0.983 for permeability. The Mean Absolute Error (MAE) values for the output parameters were 0.472% for OMC, 0.043 g/cm³ for MDD, and 0.183 for permeability, while the Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) values were 3.10%, 2.40%, and 4.67% for all output parameters.

metadata.title: Finite Screen Spacetime: Entropy-Response Geometry from Causal-Screen Ledgers
metadata.description:
FDS--G1 Complete Series v1.2 presents a finite-screen entropy-response framework for gravity and late-time dark-sector residuals. The series develops Finite Screen Spacetime as a bridge program in which the primitive gravitational object is a finite causal-screen entropy ledger, and geometry is treated as the integrable hydrodynamic response envelope of that ledger.

This v1.2 release is a full body-integrated synchronization release. Its main increment is the integration of the theory-hardening architecture across the Core, Companion, and D-series papers, together with the completed medium-prior 8-seed production-refined nested-evidence audit and the finalized KiDS BandPower EE+nE diagnostic bridge. It does not claim final cosmological confirmation, full survey-grade validation, a completed full 3×2pt result, or a completed microscopic derivation of the finite-screen realization.

The archive includes the Core paper, Companions A--F, the G1DE-M3/4 / G1fit-real data-confrontation paper, and the D0--D10 dark-sector closure sequence. Together these papers formulate the finite-screen response chain, screen statistical mechanics, stochastic screen transition dynamics, scalar-capacity EFT interface, likelihood-ready falsification tests, horizon occupancy residuals, holographic dictionaries, modular anomaly-inflow calibration targets, finite-access sheaf geometry, the 3/4 projection law, non-equilibrium screen entropy, the screen influence functional, Weyl-normalized Ward completion, maximum-caliber memory quotient, finite Markov-screen realization, metric-envelope reconstruction, obstruction exact-sequence classification, and the minimal Weyl-branch bootstrap behind G1DE-M3/4.

The current leading empirical dark-sector branch is the projection-locked G1DE-M3/4 model. Its locked late-time residual pattern is:

s < 3,   μ(a,k) = 1,   Σ(a,k) − 1 = −(3/4)(3 − s) RbH(a),   RbH(1) = 1.

Here RbH(a), also denoted Xb(a) in theory/code-facing notation, is the production-normalized horizon-response output shape. It is not assumed to be a uniquely identified microscopic source--kernel factorization. D6 formalizes this output/lift distinction through a memory quotient and maximum-caliber horizontal lift, while D7 gives a finite-state detailed-balance Markov-screen realization prototype for an admissible passive lift.

A completed medium-prior 8-seed production-refined nested-evidence audit gives the canonical evidence hierarchy:

M3/4 > Mκ > const-Σ > G1DE-2 > G1DE-1 > CPL > ΛCDM.

All seven models share the same medium-prior configuration and eight independent seeds with dlogZ = 0.1. In this production-refined audit, the locked M3/4 branch exceeds the free-κ control by Δlog Z = 0.856 (B ≃ 2.35), constant-Σ by Δlog Z = 1.775 (B ≃ 5.90), G1DE-2 by Δlog Z = 6.523 (B ≃ 6.8 × 102), the growth-only negative control G1DE-1 by Δlog Z = 7.402 (B ≃ 1.65 × 103), CPL by Δlog Z = 9.603 (B ≃ 1.5 × 104), and ΛCDM by Δlog Z = 11.884 (B ≃ 1.45 × 105).

The earlier mixed-provenance raw audit and the earlier three-seed exact-pilot audit are retained only for provenance. They have been superseded for model ranking by the medium-prior 8-seed production-refined table reported in this release.

The v1.2 theory-hardening layer integrates explicitly marked modules for finite-ledger representation, causal-measurement protocol, retarded finite-response kernels, modular-response targets, entropy-admissibility filters, and mainstream μ/Σ production-interface comparisons. Each new theoretical item is marked by claim type: definition, admissibility rule, conditional theorem target, open lemma, empirical protocol, or falsification / demotion rule. These additions harden the architecture but do not complete the microscopic derivation of the screen ledger, the D0 finite-index / boost modular calibration route, the modular derivation of the 3/4 coefficient, or the unique source--kernel realization of RbH(a).

v1.2 also records the completed KiDS BandPower EE+nE diagnostic bridge. This diagnostic track validates the compressed-space KiDS BandPower product/covariance/order bridge for the EE+nE subspace, including BandPower PeeE projector validation, PneE density-kernel smoke tests, local diagnostic refits, deterministic no-noise mock recovery, noisy mock screening, nuisance-prior robustness, prior-regularized refits, and toy nn-closure diagnostics. These results support the interpretation that the EE+nE amplitude degeneracy is driven by the missing clustering channel, but they remain diagnostic only.

The KiDS BandPower track is not a completed full 3×2pt likelihood, not production evidence, and not a final cosmological constraint. Phase 5 sourcing audits found that a full 300×300 BandPower covariance exists and that its first 200×200 block matches the validated EE+nE covariance, but no usable real Pnn / clustering data vector is locally available in the required BandPower format. Therefore full 3×2pt validation remains blocked pending a real nn/Pnn clustering vector or catalog-level recomputation.

The accompanying replication kit has been upgraded to v1.2. It adds production_evidence_v1_2/ with the production-refined nested-evidence runners, configs, per-seed JSON outputs, generated Table 3 CSV, summary files, manifests, checksums, and validation scripts. It also adds kids_bandpower_eene_v1_2/ with KiDS BandPower EE+nE diagnostic scripts, configs, output summaries, data-manifest notes, and claim-boundary documentation, plus phase5_nn_sourcing/ documenting why the full 3×2pt result remains blocked. The v1.1 replication-kit contents are retained for provenance; the v1.2 production-refined audit supersedes the older three-seed audit for current model ranking.

The purpose of this release is to provide a timestamped, internally organized, falsifiable research archive for the FDS--G1 finite-screen gravity program and the G1DE-M3/4 dark-sector branch. The framework is conditional on stated bridge assumptions and includes explicit demotion criteria: failure of the 3/4 projection lock, decisive production-level preference for constant-Σ or binned-Σ(z), unsuppressed μ-channel response, requirement of a free A(a,k), systematic mock-injection false positives, or loss to CPL / ΛCDM under production-level evidence would weaken or demote the relevant branch.

D8--D10 extend the earlier D0--D7 closure sequence without replacing it. D8 reconstructs a universal metric envelope from finite causal access and entropy-density data; D9 classifies admissible residuals through an obstruction exact sequence and no-free-dark-sector rule; D10 proves the minimal Weyl-branch structure behind the projection-locked G1DE-M3/4 model under finite-description, no-free-A(a,k), Ward-suppressed Ricci leakage, passive memory-shape, and isotropic optical-compliance assumptions. The production-refined evidence result supports the data-facing output branch; it does not prove the microscopic screen realization or production-confirm the unique RbH(a) source shape.

Recommended entry points are: Core for the finite-screen entropy-response architecture, G1DE-M3/4 / G1fit-real for the production-refined evidence comparison, Companion D for likelihood-ready falsification and evidence-layer stratification, Companion E for horizon occupancy residuals, D0 for the modular calibration route, D2 for the 3/4 projection law, D5 for Weyl-normalized production and identifiability rules, D6 for memory-output identifiability, D7 for the finite Markov-screen realization prototype, D8--D10 for the reconstruction, obstruction, and minimal Weyl-branch bootstrap layer, and Section 20 for the KiDS BandPower compressed-space bridge and full 3×2pt roadmap.

Official website: distinctiontheory.org
Public portal for the start guide, papers, claim status, failure registry, prior-art boundary, and citation resources.

Canonical GitHub repository:
https://github.com/yiningwu-research/Distinction-Theory

Replication kit:
https://github.com/yiningwu-research/Distinction-Theory/tree/main/papers/FDS_G1/replication_kit

metadata.title: An effective worldvolume action for the information world-tube: dynamical completion, a closed-timelike-curve dissipation model, and routes to falsifiability
metadata.description: A recent series of works re-described stored quantum information as an extended information world-tube whose spacelike cross section is an area-quantized region, recovered the familiar worldline as the derived eikonal centroid of that tube, placed the discrete-spacetime information network of loop quantum gravity (LQG) and a semiconductor quantum-dot array in a common renormalization-group universality class, and classified self-consistent closed-timelike-curve (CTC) histories into two families. Three fronts were explicitly left open: the dynamics of the tube, the computation of the LQG-side universality invariants, and an empirical signature. Here we take steps on all three, analytically and programmatically. First, we propose an effective worldvolume action—a Nambu–Goto/Dirac membrane term supplemented by an area–information constraint—whose extremization makes the world-tube a worldvolume of stationary area, and whose thin-tube (eikonal) reduction returns, at leading order, the timelike centroid geodesic and the Ehrenfest relation previously posited only kinematically; the leading correction couples the centroid to the background curvature through the cross-sectional quadrupole, in the manner of an extended body. Second, we record a concrete protocol for extracting the topological entanglement entropy and the entanglement spectrum from spin-network reduced density matrices, turning an unfilled component of the universality vector into a well-defined numerical task. Third, we solve a minimal single-qubit model of the post-selected “rewind” route, obtaining a closed-form Landauer entropy production that is monotone in the rewind parameter and that reproduces the loss of retained future memory. We then identify a parametric, presently inaccessible high-energy-scattering signature of the finite transverse cross section, and we set out the remaining numerical and experimental steps as a research program. Every result below is an analytical proposal or a protocol; we make no claim of numerical or experimental confirmation, and we stress that the framework remains a theoretical proposal.

data.title: An effective worldvolume action for the information world-tube: dynamical completion, a closed-timelike-curve dissipation model, and routes to falsifiability

metadata.title: A Winged Hybrid-Electric Flying Car Architecture with Reinforced Lightweight Structure: A First-Order Aerodynamic, Energy, Thermal, Rotor, Structural, and Stability Simulation Study
metadata.description: This article presents a first-order theoretical and simulation-based study of a winged hybrid-electric flying car concept that combines ground electric-vehicle operation, vertical take-off and landing assistance, wingborne cruise, reinforced lightweight structure, and a more-electric hybrid propulsion strategy. The model is intentionally framed as a pre-design simulation rather than a construction manual or an airworthiness-certified aircraft design. Standard aerodynamic and energy relations are used to estimate lift, drag, rotor induced power, road electric power, battery state of charge, fuel-cell/generator equivalent energy use, battery temperature, rotor efficiency, structural factor of safety, emergency landing reserve, motor/generator efficiency, center of gravity, and static stability. The final lightweight reinforced model reduces the reinforced mass from 1030 kg to 969 kg while retaining a 4g structural factor of safety of approximately 2.54. The calculated best lift-to-drag operating region occurs near 6.8 degrees angle of attack, with a maximum L/D ratio of approximately 11.86 in the simplified aerodynamic sweep. The energy model estimates a final battery state of charge of 82.89%, a final fuel reserve of 96.66%, a safe-landing reserve margin of 58.25 kWh, and an estimated combined road-plus-air mission distance of 53.07 km. These results indicate internal consistency between the proposed architecture and first-order physical constraints, while also identifying that real certification would require computational fluid dynamics, wind-tunnel testing, finite-element structural analysis, battery thermal management validation, rotor test-stand mapping, control-law verification, and regulated flight testing.

data.title: A Winged Hybrid-Electric Flying Car Architecture with Reinforced Lightweight Structure: A First-Order Aerodynamic, Energy, Thermal, Rotor, Structural, and Stability Simulation Study

metadata.title: Supplementary material for "Improved regional mass change estimation using Slepian functions in a Kalman filter framework"
metadata.description:
This is the supplementary material for the article "Improved regional mass change estimation using Slepian functions in a Kalman filter framework" to be published in IAG Symposia Series (Springer).
 
The supplementary material contains:
- A zip file ("slepian-functions") with figures of the Slepian functions created for the studied region of central Europe and using a buffer width of 600 km.
  • Slepian functions 1 to 156 are shown as those have an eigenvalue above 0.001, but only Slepian functions up to a rank of 90 are used.
  • The number of the Slepian function k is shown in the upper left and the associated eigenvalue in the bottom right of each figure.
  • The number of the Slepian function is also indicated in the filename: slepian_nr"k"_centraleurope.png
  • All figures use the same colormap.
 
- A zip file ("combined-data_all-months") with figures of all months showing:
  • the reference data (data without noise) (upper left),
  • the noisy data (upper right),
  • the Slepian-Kalman-filter results using a buffer width of 600 km (lower left),
  • the Slepian-Kalman-filter results using a buffer width of 900 km (lower right).
  • The year and month is shown in the bottom left of each sub-figure.
  • The year and month is also indicated in the filename: centraleurope_"year"-"month".png
  • All figures use the same colormap.

metadata.title: The Totality Theorem: Resolution of the Closed Universe Paradox through Observer-Universe Equivalence
metadata.description:
Recent work in quantum gravity has revealed that closed universes appear to admit only a one-dimensional Hilbert space, implying zero information content. The leading resolution by Harlow, Usatyuk, and Zhao (arXiv:2501.02359, January 2025), featured in Quanta Magazine (November 2025), introduces observers as external additions to restore complexity. We demonstrate that this approach is self-contradictory: it resolves a closed universe problem by opening the universe, introducing boundaries in a system defined by their absence.

We present a fundamentally different resolution. By proving that the open/closed distinction is itself a declaration and establishing observer-universe equivalence (O ≡ U), we derive the Totality Theorem: T = O + U = 1. The one-state result is not a paradox but a correct description of completeness: Shannon entropy H = 0 indicates full knowledge, not emptiness. We show that dimensionality itself is an artifact of partition, not a feature of reality.

From three relations alone — T = 1, O ≡ U, dO = −dU — and a single structural principle (the Law of Identity A = A generates the binary partition A + ¬A = 1 with unique fixed point A = ¬A = 0.5), we resolve problems across every foundational domain: the unification of the four laws of thermodynamics as facets of a single identity, the black hole information paradox, the cosmological constant discrepancy, the Collatz and twin prime conjectures, Wigner's 67-year mystery of mathematical effectiveness, and the dissolution of Gödel's incompleteness as a property of notation rather than truth. All results derive from A = A.

data.title: The Totality Theorem: Resolution of the Closed Universe Paradox through Observer-Universe Equivalence

metadata.title: Beyond Spacetime: How Time Generates Gravity
metadata.description:
Background:

The relationship between space, time, energy, and gravity remains a fundamental open question in theoretical physics. General relativity describes gravity as spacetime curvature sourced by energy-matter, but time is treated as a passive parameter rather than an active gravitational contributor.

Material and methods:

We propose a novel algebraic framework based on the dimensionally consistent equation $S = \frac{8\pi G}{c^4} E + c \cdot t$, where $S$ is spatial length, $E$ energy, and $t$ time. This equation is derived by introducing dimensional correction factors to the naive relation $S = E + t$. We analyze its consequences through division by powers of $c$ and by volume $V$, leading to curvature-like quantities and an energy density formulation.

Results:

The division by $c^3$ yields $\frac{S}{c^3} = \frac{8\pi G}{c^5} m + g$ with $g = t/c^2$, suggesting that time generates a gravitational field independent of mass. The condition $(c \cdot t)/V = R/2 - \Lambda$ unifies time with the Ricci scalar $R$ and the cosmological constant $\Lambda$, implying $\Lambda = (c \cdot t)/V$ in vacuum.

Conclusion:

This framework proposes that time itself acts as an independent source of gravity, potentially resolving the cosmological constant problem and the problem of time in quantum gravity. Further theoretical and experimental validation is required.

data.title: Beyond Spacetime: How Time Generates Gravity
